text: Lecture	  5	  –	  Muscle	  Physiology	   	   Learning	  outcomes	   • Organization	  of	  muscles	  fibres	   • Excitation-­‐contraction	  coupling	  	   • Twitch,	  summation	  and	  tetanus	   • Sliding	  filament	  theory	   • Length-­‐tension	  relationship	   • Force-­‐velocity-­‐power	  relationship	  	   • Muscle	  damage	  	   • Ageing	  	   	   • 30-­‐40%	  of	  body	  weight	   • Functions:	   o Movement	   o Respiration	   o Metabolism	  (eg	  thermogenesis)	   o Endocrine	  (eg	  myokines)	   • Losing	  muscle	  mass	  and	  function	  with	  disease	  and	  ageing	   • Gain	  muscle	  mass	  (hypertrophy)	  and	  function	  with	  exercise	   	   Structural	  Organisation	   • Muscle	   	   	   	   	   	   (Epimysium)	   • Bundle	  of	  muscle	  fibres	  	   	   	   (Perimysium)	   (Endomysium	  –	  btwn	  fibres	  containing	  vessels,	  nerves)	   • Muscle	  Fibre	   	   	   	   	   (Sarcolemma	  –	  the	  cell	  membrane)	   o Myofibrils	    Sarcomeres	   • Myofilaments:	  Myosin,	  actin	  etc	   o Transverse	  tubules	  –	  invaginations	  of	  the	  sarcolemma	  that	  convey	  Action	  Potentials	   	   Sarcomere	   • The	  structural	  and	  contractile	  unit	  of	  a	  myofibril.	   • Many	  are	  laid	  end-­‐to-­‐end	  or	  next	  to	  each	  other	  to	  inc	  length	   and	  diameter	  of	  myofibril. 	   • Sarcomere	  –	  Defined	  as	  from	  one	  Z-­‐disc	  to	  another	  Z-­‐disc	  	   o Z	  disc	  –	  appears	  as	  dark	  bands	  in	  image.	   o Contains	  structural	  protein	  to	  hold	  actin	  and	  myosin	   together	   o Has	  signalling	  pathways	  for	  gene	  regulation	  eg	   protein	  production.	   Regions	   • I	  band	  –	  light,	  contains	  thin	  filaments	  (actin)	   • A	  band	  –	  dark,	  thick	  (myosin)	  and	  thin	  (actin)	  filaments	  	   The Sarcomere • Sarcomere = from Z disc to Z disc • Z disc anchors thin filaments & titin • M line anchors thick filament • Dark A band is thick filament region Lighter, central H band area contains no thin filaments • Light I band is thin filament region Bisected by Z disc • H	  band	  –	  lighter	  zone	  in	  the	  A	  band,	  where	  myosin	  and	  actin	  aren’t	  interacting,	  only	  thick	  filaments	   • M	  line	  –	  in	  the	  middle	  of	  the	  h	  band.	  Structural	  proteins	  to	  ensure	  alignment	  of	  proteins.	   • Titin	  –	  elastic	  protein	  that	  connects	  z	  line	  to	  myosin,	  near	  m	  line.	   o Assists	  in	  maintaining	  stability/alignment	  of	  the	  myofibril	   o Also	  has	  signalling	  roles	   • Desmin	  –	  connects	  the	  z	  disk	  to	  the	  cytoskeleton	  of	  the	  myofibril.	   o Winds	  around	  the	  Z	  disc	  to	  support	  it	   	   Myosin	  Filament	  (Thick)	   • Light	  meromyosin	  and	  Heavy	  meromyosin	  wound	  around	  each	  other	   • S1	  head	  –	  does	  the	  contractile	  work	   • S2	  head	   • flexible	  neck	  	   • Many	  myosin	  wound	  around	  each	  other	  make	  up	  the	  thick	  filament	   	   Actin	  Filaments	  (Thin)	   • Double	  helical	  strand	  	   • Around	  actin	  is	  tropomyosin,	  a	  regulatory	  protein	  that	  prevents	  S1	  head	  of	  myosin	  from	  binding.	   • Troponin	  T,	  of	  the	  troponin	  complex,	  binds	  to	  tropomyosin.	   • The	  binding	  of	  Ca2+	  to	  Troponin	  C	  causes	  a	  conformational	  change	  that	  moves	  Troponin	  I.	  The	   conformational	  change	  means	  that	  tropomyosin	  no	   longer	  binds	  to	  actin,	  allowing	  myosin	  to	  bind	  actin.	   	   	   	   	   	   Sliding	  Filament	  Theory	   • Based	  on	  observations	  of	  varying	  A/I	  bands	  with	  different	  degrees	  of	  muscle	  stretch	  	   • Muscle	  contraction	  occurs	  as	  a	  result	  of	  the	  filaments	  sliding	  between	  each	  other	  to	  shorten	  the	  muscle	  	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Thick (myosin) Filaments Thin (actin) Filaments Myosin Thick filament LMM HMM Light meromyosin Heavy meromyosin S1 – globular heads S2 - flexible Hundreds of myosin molecules form a thick filament S1 S2 150 nm Regulatory Proteins Tropomyosin: Spans 7 actin subunits Stabilises actin filament Covers myosin binding site Troponin: 3 subunits Troponin C – binds calcium Troponin T – linked to tropomyosin Troponin I – inhibitory on actin filament 1 m long, 5-10 nm wide Double-helical strand ~400 G actin molecules Binds S1 segment of myosin Cross	  Bridge	  Cycling	  (http://books.google.com/books?id=gOmpysGBC90C)	   • Z	  disks	  move	  towards	  each	  other,	  I	  band	  shortens.	   • Myosin	  head	  has	  an	  ATPase	  -­‐	  ATP	  is	  bound	  and	  converted	  to	  ADP,	  there	  is	  potential	  energy	  in	  the	  myosin	  	   • Myosin	  binds	  to	  actin	  strand,	  use	  of	  stored	  energy	  to	  pull	  filament	  	   • As	  long	  as	  ATP	  supply	  is	  maintained,	  and	  Ca2+	  is	  present	  to	  expose	  the	  binding	  sites,	  contraction	  will	   occur	   o Rigor	  mortis	  occurs	  when	  myosin-­‐actin	  remain	  bound	  as	  no	  more	  ATP	   • Need	  millions	  of	  sarcomeres	  to	  produce	  enough	  force	  for	  muscle	  contraction	   	   Excitation-­‐Contraction	  Coupling	   1. Surface	  membrane	  pumps	  maintain	  resting	  potential	   2. AP	  causes	  Ca2+	  influx	  into	  T	  tubules	  (membrane	   structures	  containing	  ECF)	   3. Voltage	  gated	  DHP	  (dihydropuridine)	  channels	  open	   letting	  Ca2+	  into	  the	  cell	   4. Via	  CICR,	  Ca2+	  dependent	  RyR	  gates	  on	  the	  terminal	   cisternae	  of	  SR	  open	  up,	  flooding	  the	  cell	  with	  Ca2+	   • Terminal	  cisternae	  –	  large	  chambers	  at	  ends	  of	   tubule	  making	  up	  SR	   5. Ca2+	  binds	  to	  troponin	  C	  causing	  conformational	  change	   6. Tropomyosin	  moves	  and	  exposes	  myosin	  binding	  sites	   7. Cross	  bridge	  cycling	   8. Ca2+	  pumped	  back	  into	  SR.	   Muscle	  weakness	  due	  to	  disruption	  at	  any	  stage	   	   	   Excitation-Contraction Coupling 1. Action potential spreads over sarcolemma and enters T-tubules 2. Voltage-gated channels (DHPR) open in T-tubules causing calcium gates(RyR) to open in SR 3. Calcium released by SR binds to troponin-C 4. Troponin-tropomyosin complex changes shape and exposes active sites on actin 5. Myosin-actin interaction to form crossbridges = force production, muscle shortening 6. Calcium pumped back into SR Force	  Summation	   • Twitch:	  single	  contraction	  by	  an	  AP.	   • Summation:	  sustained	  contraction	  due	  to	  multiple	  APs	  such	  that	  repolarisation	  is	  not	  possible.	   • Tetanus:	  sustained	  contraction	  that	  generates	  the	  maximum	  force	  possible.	   • This	  is	  all	  dependent	  on	  the	  concentration	  of	  Ca2+	  being	  maintained	  by	  neurological	  stimuli.	   Increased	  frequency	  of	  stimulation	  =	  increased	  force.	   	   Types	  of	  muscle	  actions	   • Isometric	  (Same	  length)	   o Force	  generated	  is	  enough	  to	  keep	  muscle	  still	  against	  an	  external	  weight	   • Concentric	  (Shortening)	   o Force	  generated	  will	  contract	  muscle	  against	  an	  external	  weight	   • Eccentric	  (Lengthening)	   o Force	  generated	  will	  extend	  the	  muscle	  against	   an	  external	  weight.	  Ie	  weight	  is	  greater	  than	   force	  generated.	   	   (External)	  Force-­‐Velocity	  Relationship	   • At	  a	  fixed	  force,	  muscle	  velocity	  is	  inversely	  proportional	   to	  the	  load	  it	  is	  bearing.	   o i.e.	  heavy	  load,	  slow	  shortening	  velocity	  and	   vice	  versa.	   o At	  the	  max	  force/load	  (Po)	  velocity	  is	  0	  is	   isometric	  contraction	   o When	  force/load	  is	  0,	  velocity	  is	  max	  (Vmax)	   o How	  much	  force	  muscle	  generates	  and	  how	  quickly	  it	  can	  shorten.	   • Vmax	  is	  dependent	  on	  sarcomeres	  in	  series.	  More	  =	  greater	  velocity	   • Po	  is	  dependent	  on	  the	  total	  number	  of	  sarcomere	  in	  a	  muscle	  fibre	  (sacromeres	  in	  parallel).	  We	  can	   increase	  the	  number	  by	  making	  the	  fibre	  thicker,	  ie	  more	  myofibrils.	  This	  is	  what	  occurs	  under	  exercise	   hypertrophy.	   	   	   	   External	  Force,	  Velocity	  and	  Power	   • Evidently,	  power	  is	  going	  to	  be	  an	  optimum	  curve.	   • Power	  =	  Force	  x	  Velocity	  	   o (See	  slides	  for	  derivation)	  	   o P	  =	  WD/t	  =	  F.d/t	  =	  FV	   • At	  max	  external	  force,	  velocity	  is	  compromised,	  ie	   your	  muscle	  doesn’t	  move.	  So,	  Power	  =	  0	   • At	  max	  velocity	  there	  is	  no	  external	  force,	  So,	  Power	   =	  0.	   • Optimum	  is	  in	  between	  somewhere.	   	   	   	   Each myofibril contributes to force production Force-Velocity Relationship Muscle shortens faster when contracting against a smaller load A.V. Hill (1938) - The Heat of Shortening and the Dynamic Constants of Muscle Vmax depends on the length of myofibrils (number of sarcomeres) Each sarcomere contributes to length change/unit time Max Force (Po) @ 0 Velocity Max Velocity (Vmax) @ 0 Force Po depends on the cross-sectional area of muscle fibres Force-Velocity and Power Power = WD t = F x d t = F x Vel WD – work done t – time F – force d – displacement Max Max Velocity Force Power Force Power = 0 at max Force (Vel = 0) Power = 0 at max Vel (Force = 0) Muscles don’t contract at a constant velocity or against a constant load Power – rate at which ‘work’ is done – explosive activities Each myofibril contributes to force production Force-Velocity Relationship Muscle shortens faster when contracting against a smaller load A.V. Hill (1938) - The Heat of Shortening and the Dynamic Constants of Muscle Vmax depends on the length of myofibrils (number of sarcomeres) Each sarcomere contributes to length change/unit time Max Force (Po) @ 0 Velocity Max Velocity (Vmax) @ 0 Force Po depends on the cross-sectional area of muscle fibres 	   Length-­‐Tension	  Relationship	   • Active	  force	  is	  determined	  by	  number	  of	  actin-­‐myosin	   interactions	  –	  ie	  the	  overlapping	  of	  the	  two	  filaments.	   • When	  the	  muscle	  is	  stretched	  (eg	  preload	  on	  heart)	   the	  total	  force	  is	  greater	  due	  to	  passive	  forces	   generated	  by	  elastic	  protein	  such	  as	  Titan	  and	   Desmin.	  	   o Passive	  forces	  allow	  for	  an	  increasing	   proportion	  of	  total	  force	  as	  length	  increases	   	   Stretch	  induced	  (Eccentric)	  damage	   • Repeated	  stretch	  eventually	  overstretched	  structural	   proteins	  such	  as	  Titin	  etc.	   • Thus	  the	  sarcomere	  loses	  is	  orderly	  arrangement,	   thus	  impaired	  muscle	  function.	   • Damage	  may	  be	  extensive	  and	  effects	  SR,	  T	  tubules	  etc.	   • Much	  of	  the	  weakness	  is	  due	  to	  damaged	  excitation	  contraction	  coupling	  (ECC)	  failure.	   	   Sarcopenia	   • Age	  related	  loss	  of	  muscle	  function.	   o 60%	  loss	  of	  muscle	  fibres,	  30-­‐40%	  loss	  of	  cross-­‐sectional	  area	   o reduced	  strength	  (more	  than	  can	  be	  attributed	  to	  loss	  of	  muscle	  mass,	  one	  of	  factors	  would	  be	   sarcomere	  disorder)	   o Loss	  in	  both	  cross	  sectional	  area	  and	  length	  of	  myofibrils	  (#	  of	  sarcomeres	  in	  series)	   o reduced	  velocity	  of	  muscle	  contraction	  (impaired	  Ca2+	  release	  and	  uptake)	   • Some	  is	  muscle	  wasting	  (loss	  of	  myofibrils)	  but	  much	  is	  loss	  of	  ECC	  mechanisms	   • Increased	  fat	  and	  CT	  composition	  in	  muscle.	   	   Length-tension relationship The image cannot be display ed. Active force production determined by number of crossbridges actin-myosin filament overlap Total force increases at longer muscle lengths due to increased passive force (ie strain of non- contractile proteins such as titin)
